ai-readiness-api-description.description: AI agents use the API-level description to decide whether the API is relevant to the user's goal. A missing or very short description causes agents to misidentify the API's purpose or skip it entirely.
ai-readiness-api-description.fixSuggestion: Add info.description (minimum 50 characters) covering: what the API does, its intended consumers, and primary use cases.

ai-readiness-api-contact.description: Contact information helps agents surface support paths when they encounter undocumented errors or unexpected behavior.
ai-readiness-api-contact.fixSuggestion: Add an info.contact object with at least name and email or url fields.

ai-readiness-server-description.description: Without server descriptions agents cannot distinguish production from staging endpoints, risking unintended data writes to the wrong environment.
ai-readiness-server-description.fixSuggestion: Add a description to each servers[] entry specifying the environment (e.g., "Production API", "Staging API").

ai-readiness-operation-summary.description: Agents use the operation summary as the primary signal for tool selection. A missing summary forces the agent to guess from the HTTP method and path, leading to incorrect tool choices and hallucinated calls.
ai-readiness-operation-summary.fixSuggestion: Add a concise summary starting with an imperative verb that describes the business action, e.g. "Create a new order" or "List all invoices by status".

ai-readiness-callback-operation-summary.description: Missing callback summaries prevent agents from understanding webhook payloads they receive in async flows, causing incorrect response handling.
ai-readiness-callback-operation-summary.fixSuggestion: Add a summary to the callback operation describing the event it represents, e.g. "Payment completed notification".

ai-readiness-webhook-operation-summary.description: Without a webhook summary agents cannot identify what triggered the webhook or how to process the payload.
ai-readiness-webhook-operation-summary.fixSuggestion: Add a summary to the webhook operation, e.g. "Order status change event".

ai-readiness-path-item-summary.description: Path-level summaries provide route-level context that helps agents understand the resource before reading individual operations.
ai-readiness-path-item-summary.fixSuggestion: Add a summary field to the path item describing the resource, e.g. "Order management endpoints".

ai-readiness-path-item-description.description: Path-level descriptions let agents understand resource semantics, ownership boundaries, and access rules before selecting an operation.
ai-readiness-path-item-description.fixSuggestion: Add a description field to the path item covering the resource's purpose, ownership model, and key constraints.

ai-readiness-operation-description.description: Operation descriptions carry critical context: preconditions, side effects, draft vs. finalized state, and workflow position. Agents that skip steps or invoke operations in the wrong order do so because descriptions are absent.
ai-readiness-operation-description.fixSuggestion: Add a description (minimum 30 characters) covering: preconditions, side effects, whether the result is draft or finalized, required permissions, and where this operation sits in the workflow.

ai-readiness-operation-id.description: operationId is how agents reference tools. Without it, the agent cannot reliably call the operation and may fabricate or omit it entirely.
ai-readiness-operation-id.fixSuggestion: Add an operationId using verb-noun format based on the HTTP method and resource, e.g. POST /orders → "createOrder", GET /invoices/{id} → "getInvoiceById".

ai-readiness-operation-id-casing.description: Inconsistent operationId casing (mixing camelCase and snake_case) confuses agents that must reference operations by name across multi-step workflows.
ai-readiness-operation-id-casing.fixSuggestion: Choose one casing style (camelCase recommended) and rename all operationIds to match consistently.

ai-readiness-operation-id-unique.description: Duplicate operationIds cause agents to call the wrong operation. When two operations share an ID, tool selection is non-deterministic.
ai-readiness-operation-id-unique.fixSuggestion: Rename the duplicate operationId to be unique. Suffix with a disambiguating term, e.g. "getOrderById" vs "getOrderSummaryById".

ai-readiness-operation-tags.description: Tags group operations by domain. Without tags, agents cannot efficiently discover which operations belong to the same resource family.
ai-readiness-operation-tags.fixSuggestion: Add at least one tag per operation matching the resource name, e.g. "orders", "invoices", "users".

ai-readiness-parameter-description.description: Agents cannot infer what value to pass for a parameter without a description. Missing descriptions lead to hallucinated or empty values.
ai-readiness-parameter-description.fixSuggestion: Add a description explaining the parameter's purpose, valid values, and business meaning. For IDs, state where the value can be obtained.

ai-readiness-parameter-description-lengt.description: A description shorter than 15 characters (e.g., "The ID") gives agents no actionable context — they will guess or omit the value.
ai-readiness-parameter-description-lengt.fixSuggestion: Expand the description to at least 15 characters. Include valid values, format, and sourcing information.

ai-readiness-parameter-example.description: Examples anchor agents to realistic values. Without them, agents generate synthetic values that may fail validation or produce unexpected results.
ai-readiness-parameter-example.fixSuggestion: Add an example or examples field to the parameter with a realistic value representative of actual API usage.

ai-readiness-path-parameter-example.description: Path-level parameter examples help agents construct valid URLs. Without them, agents may fabricate IDs that do not exist in the system.
ai-readiness-path-parameter-example.fixSuggestion: Add an example field to path-level parameters with a realistic value, e.g. example: "ord_12345".

ai-readiness-parameter-content-example.description: Parameters using content-typed media need examples to help agents serialize the value correctly for the given content type.
ai-readiness-parameter-content-example.fixSuggestion: Add an example or examples field inside the content media type object.

ai-readiness-path-parameter-content-exam.description: Path-level parameters that use content types need examples to help agents construct valid URLs with correctly formatted values.
ai-readiness-path-parameter-content-exam.fixSuggestion: Add an example or examples field inside the path-level parameter's content media type object.

ai-readiness-request-body-description.description: Without a request body description, agents do not understand what the payload represents, its constraints, or how it relates to the operation's outcome.
ai-readiness-request-body-description.fixSuggestion: Add a description to the requestBody explaining what the payload contains, any required business rules, and how it affects the response.

ai-readiness-request-body-example.description: Request body examples give agents a concrete template for payload construction. Without them, agents produce structurally valid but semantically wrong payloads.
ai-readiness-request-body-example.fixSuggestion: Add an example or examples field inside the requestBody content media type with a complete, realistic payload.

ai-readiness-request-body-schema-typed.description: Without an explicit type on the request body schema, agents cannot determine how to construct the payload and may produce invalid JSON structures.
ai-readiness-request-body-schema-typed.fixSuggestion: Add type: object (or the correct type) to the request body schema.

ai-readiness-request-body-schema-require.description: Without a required array, agents treat all properties as optional and may omit mandatory fields, causing validation failures.
ai-readiness-request-body-schema-require.fixSuggestion: Add a required array listing all mandatory property names to the request body schema, e.g. required: [name, email].

ai-readiness-response-description.description: Response descriptions tell agents what a status code means in business terms. Without them, agents cannot distinguish a 200 (resource created) from a 200 (operation queued for async processing).
ai-readiness-response-description.fixSuggestion: Add a description to each response explaining its business meaning and any follow-up actions the agent should take.

ai-readiness-response-example.description: Response examples let agents anticipate response structure and extract IDs or tokens needed for subsequent workflow steps.
ai-readiness-response-example.fixSuggestion: Add an example or examples field to each success response content type with a realistic payload including all key fields.

ai-readiness-response-header-example.description: Response header examples help agents understand the format of values like pagination cursors, rate limit tokens, or correlation IDs.
ai-readiness-response-header-example.fixSuggestion: Add an example field to each response header with a realistic value.

ai-readiness-response-content-type.description: Responses without a declared content type leave agents unable to deserialize the body correctly, leading to parsing errors or ignored payloads.
ai-readiness-response-content-type.fixSuggestion: Add a content field to the response with at least application/json as the key and an empty schema as the value.

ai-readiness-response-schema-typed.description: Without an explicit type on the response schema, agents cannot reliably parse the response or extract fields for use in subsequent steps.
ai-readiness-response-schema-typed.fixSuggestion: Add type: object (or the correct type) to the response schema.

ai-readiness-success-response-content.description: A 2xx response without content leaves agents with no payload to process. They cannot extract data needed for subsequent workflow steps.
ai-readiness-success-response-content.fixSuggestion: Add a content field with application/json and at least a minimal schema definition to the success response.

ai-readiness-success-response-json-schem.description: Without a schema on the JSON success response, agents cannot understand the structure of the payload they receive.
ai-readiness-success-response-json-schem.fixSuggestion: Add a schema object inside the application/json content of the 2xx response.

ai-readiness-error-response-content.description: Error responses without content give agents no information about why the request failed, preventing self-correction.
ai-readiness-error-response-content.fixSuggestion: Add a content field with application/json to all 4xx/5xx responses, pointing to an error schema.

ai-readiness-error-response-json-schema.description: Without a schema on error response JSON content, agents cannot parse the error body to extract error codes, messages, or field-level details.
ai-readiness-error-response-json-schema.fixSuggestion: Add a schema to the application/json content of each error response. At minimum define message and code properties.

ai-readiness-success-response.description: Operations without a defined 2xx response leave agents uncertain whether the call succeeded. They cannot chain the result into subsequent steps.
ai-readiness-success-response.fixSuggestion: Add at least one 2xx response (200, 201, 202, or 204) with a description and content schema.

ai-readiness-error-responses-4xx.description: Without defined 4xx responses, agents cannot distinguish authentication failures (401) from authorization failures (403) or validation errors (422), making self-correction impossible.
ai-readiness-error-responses-4xx.fixSuggestion: Define the relevant 4xx responses for this operation. At minimum include 400 (bad request), 401 (unauthorized), 403 (forbidden), and 429 (rate limited). Add 404 for operations that reference a specific resource.

ai-readiness-error-responses-5xx.description: Missing 5xx definitions prevent agents from implementing retry logic. They cannot tell a transient server error from a permanent failure.
ai-readiness-error-responses-5xx.fixSuggestion: Add a 500 (internal server error) response with a description advising the caller to retry with exponential backoff.

ai-readiness-error-response-schema.description: Error response content without a schema gives agents no structure to parse. They cannot extract the error code or message to guide self-correction.
ai-readiness-error-response-schema.fixSuggestion: Add a schema to the error response content with at minimum message (string) and code (string) properties.

ai-readiness-error-response-description-.description: Very short error descriptions (e.g., "Unauthorized") give agents no actionable context. They cannot determine what credential is missing or how to obtain it.
ai-readiness-error-response-description-.fixSuggestion: Expand the error response description to at least 20 characters, explaining the cause and what the caller should do to resolve it.

ai-readiness-schema-description.description: Schema descriptions give agents semantic context about what a data type represents in the business domain.
ai-readiness-schema-description.fixSuggestion: Add a description to the schema in components explaining its business meaning, constraints, and lifecycle.

ai-readiness-schema-description-length.description: A description shorter than 20 characters provides too little context for agents to understand the schema's purpose.
ai-readiness-schema-description-length.fixSuggestion: Expand the schema description to at least 20 characters.

ai-readiness-schema-title.description: Schema titles help agents identify what a reusable component represents without having to infer it from context.
ai-readiness-schema-title.fixSuggestion: Add a title field matching the schema's component name, e.g. title: Order.

ai-readiness-schema-example.description: Schema examples give agents a concrete reference for understanding what valid data looks like, reducing hallucinated field values.
ai-readiness-schema-example.fixSuggestion: Add an example or examples field to the schema with a complete, realistic instance of the data.

ai-readiness-schema-property-description.description: Properties without descriptions force agents to guess their meaning from the field name, often incorrectly for domain-specific terms.
ai-readiness-schema-property-description.fixSuggestion: Add a description to each property explaining its business meaning, valid values, and any relationship to other properties.

ai-readiness-schema-property-example.description: Property-level examples ground agents in realistic values and reduce hallucinated payloads during construction.
ai-readiness-schema-property-example.fixSuggestion: Add an example field to each property with a realistic value representative of actual data.

ai-readiness-component-header-example.description: Component header examples help agents understand the expected format of header values when they appear in responses.
ai-readiness-component-header-example.fixSuggestion: Add an example field to each component header definition.

ai-readiness-schema-enum-description.description: Enum properties without descriptions leave agents unable to understand the business meaning of each allowed value, leading to incorrect value selection.
ai-readiness-schema-enum-description.fixSuggestion: Add a description to the enum property explaining each value's meaning, e.g. "status: pending=awaiting payment, active=payment confirmed, cancelled=order voided".

ai-readiness-schema-property-type.description: Properties without explicit types allow agents to assign any type, producing payloads that fail schema validation or cause unexpected server behavior.
ai-readiness-schema-property-type.fixSuggestion: Add an explicit type field to each property (string, integer, boolean, array, object). Use format for further precision, e.g. format: uuid.

ai-readiness-parameter-schema-type.description: Parameters without explicit schema types force agents to infer the type from context, leading to incorrectly serialized query strings or path segments.
ai-readiness-parameter-schema-type.fixSuggestion: Add type: string (or the correct type) to the parameter schema. Add format if applicable.

ai-readiness-schema-string-format.description: String properties without a format modifier (e.g., date-time, email, uuid) leave agents unable to produce correctly formatted values.
ai-readiness-schema-string-format.fixSuggestion: Add a format field to string properties with semantic meaning, e.g. format: date-time, format: email, format: uuid.

ai-readiness-schema-no-empty-object.description: An object schema with no properties, additionalProperties, or composition keywords is opaque to agents. They cannot construct or validate payloads.
ai-readiness-schema-no-empty-object.fixSuggestion: Define the object's structure by adding properties, or set additionalProperties: false if the schema is intentionally closed.

ai-readiness-schema-property-no-empty-ob.description: Object-typed properties with no defined structure prevent agents from knowing what fields to populate when constructing payloads.
ai-readiness-schema-property-no-empty-ob.fixSuggestion: Define the property's structure by adding nested properties, or set additionalProperties: false if the object is intentionally schema-free.

ai-readiness-array-items-defined.description: Arrays without an items schema are ambiguous: agents cannot determine what type each element should be or how to construct valid array payloads.
ai-readiness-array-items-defined.fixSuggestion: Add an items schema to the array definition specifying the element type and structure.

ai-readiness-array-property-items-define.description: Array-typed properties without an items definition make it impossible for agents to produce correctly typed array elements.
ai-readiness-array-property-items-define.fixSuggestion: Add an items field to the array property with the element's type and any relevant schema constraints.

ai-readiness-schema-validation-constrain.description: Scalar properties without validation constraints (pattern, minLength, maxLength, minimum, maximum) allow agents to generate out-of-range values that pass schema validation but fail business rules.
ai-readiness-schema-validation-constrain.fixSuggestion: Add appropriate constraints: pattern for strings with a fixed format, minLength/maxLength for bounded strings, minimum/maximum for numeric ranges.

ai-readiness-error-schema-fields.description: Error schemas without message and code fields give agents no structured information to parse. They cannot determine what failed or how to correct it.
ai-readiness-error-schema-fields.fixSuggestion: Add message (string) and code (string) properties to all error response schemas. message explains the failure; code is a machine-readable identifier.

ai-readiness-error-schema-rfc7807.description: Error schemas that do not follow RFC 7807 (missing type, title, detail) prevent agents from using standard problem detail parsing to understand failures.
ai-readiness-error-schema-rfc7807.fixSuggestion: Add type (string URI), title (short summary), and detail (human-readable explanation) to the error schema per RFC 7807.

ai-readiness-error-schema-details.description: A details field gives agents granular context about what exactly failed, enabling targeted self-correction without retrying blindly.
ai-readiness-error-schema-details.fixSuggestion: Add a details field (array of objects) to the error schema containing field-level error information: field name, reason, and expected value.

ai-readiness-error-schema-actionable.description: 422 errors without expected and received fields leave agents unable to automatically correct malformed requests. They must guess what the correct value should be.
ai-readiness-error-schema-actionable.fixSuggestion: Add expected (what the server expected) and received (what it got) to the 422 error schema so agents can correct and retry the request immediately.

ai-readiness-429-rate-limit-headers.description: 429 responses without Retry-After or X-RateLimit-* headers leave agents unable to determine when they can safely retry. This causes retry storms or indefinite waits.
ai-readiness-429-rate-limit-headers.fixSuggestion: Add a Retry-After header (integer, seconds to wait) or X-RateLimit-Reset header to the 429 response definition.

ai-readiness-list-pagination-params.description: GET list operations without pagination parameters force agents to retrieve all results in one call, which may be too large to process or may time out.
ai-readiness-list-pagination-params.fixSuggestion: Add cursor or page and limit query parameters to GET list operations. Document the maximum value for limit and the format of the cursor.

ai-readiness-pagination-response-meta.description: List responses without pagination metadata (has_more, next_cursor, total) leave agents unable to determine whether more pages exist, causing them to stop early or loop indefinitely.
ai-readiness-pagination-response-meta.fixSuggestion: Add pagination metadata to the list response schema: has_more (boolean), next_cursor (string, the cursor for the next page), and optionally total (integer).

ai-readiness-no-interactive-auth.description: Interactive OAuth flows (implicit, authorizationCode) require a browser redirect that AI agents cannot complete. The agent will be permanently blocked from authenticating.
ai-readiness-no-interactive-auth.fixSuggestion: Replace interactive OAuth flows with non-interactive alternatives: clientCredentials for service-to-service auth, or apiKey/bearer token for agent-facing endpoints.

ai-readiness-security-defined.description: Without defined security schemes, agents have no guidance on how to authenticate. They will either skip auth entirely or attempt to guess credentials.
ai-readiness-security-defined.fixSuggestion: Add a securitySchemes object to components defining the supported auth mechanism (e.g., bearer token, API key). Reference it on operations or at the API level.

ai-readiness-security-description.description: Security scheme descriptions explain how to obtain credentials and any scope restrictions. Without them, agents cannot self-configure authentication.
ai-readiness-security-description.fixSuggestion: Add a description to each security scheme explaining how to obtain a token or key, what scopes are available, and any restrictions.

ai-readiness-security-on-mutating-ops.description: Mutating operations (POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) without explicit security declarations may be called unauthenticated by agents, causing authorization failures or unintended state changes.
ai-readiness-security-on-mutating-ops.fixSuggestion: Add a security field to each mutating operation referencing the appropriate security scheme, e.g. security: [{bearerAuth: []}].

ai-readiness-idempotency-key.description: When agents retry a POST or PATCH after a network timeout, they may create duplicate resources or apply the same mutation twice. An Idempotency-Key header prevents this.
ai-readiness-idempotency-key.fixSuggestion: Add an Idempotency-Key header parameter to POST and PATCH operations. Document that the caller must supply a unique key per logical request, and that the server deduplicates on this key.

ai-readiness-tags-description.description: Tag descriptions help agents understand what a group of operations collectively manages, aiding domain-level navigation.
ai-readiness-tags-description.fixSuggestion: Add a description to each entry in the top-level tags array explaining the domain it represents.

ai-readiness-tags-external-docs.description: External documentation links on tags allow agents to fetch deeper domain knowledge when the spec alone is insufficient.
ai-readiness-tags-external-docs.fixSuggestion: Add an externalDocs field to each tag with a url pointing to relevant documentation.

ai-readiness-discriminator.description: Polymorphic schemas (oneOf/anyOf) without a discriminator require agents to attempt each variant to find the right type, adding latency and risk of incorrect type selection.
ai-readiness-discriminator.fixSuggestion: Add a discriminator with propertyName to the oneOf/anyOf schema so agents can select the correct variant in O(1).

ai-readiness-deprecation-notice.description: Deprecated operations without an explanation leave agents calling endpoints that may be removed, and without guidance on the replacement to use instead.
ai-readiness-deprecation-notice.fixSuggestion: Add a description to the deprecated operation that includes the word "deprecated", the reason, and the replacement operation to use instead, e.g. "Deprecated: use createOrderV2 instead. This endpoint will be removed after 2025-12-01."
